rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF DAMISELA VADO
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting
rulings.Rulings: Appearance required. Issues to include: a. The accounting petition does not contain a report. b. Conservator obtained the proceeds of the life insurance policy, which appears to have been $31.970.64, and which he distributed ½ to himself and ½ to a Vinicio Escobar. c. There are no allegations as to what happened to the 80 shares of Wells Fargo stock or the $682.43 in cash, or if there is anything left in the estate now. d. Cash has not been verified. e. Cash withdrawals and creditor card payments totaling $2,100 are shown without any explanation. f. Conservator reimbursed himself $440 without any explanation. g. $1,008 was paid in error  repaid, but there is no receipt of $1,008 shown in the accounting. h. Proposed order has not been submitted. Remote appearances may be made. Please review Local Rule 14.1. The following information pertains to appearances by Zoom: WHEN ENTERING "YOUR NAME" ON ZOOM, BOTH THE FIRST AND LAST NAME, AS WELL THE CASE NUMBER, MUST BE INCLUDED. Meeting ID: 160 225 4765 Password: 514879 To appear by telephone through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252, and follow the prompts, using the meeting ID and password above.

rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF JESUS PULE
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #3, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Grant without hearing as there were no further objections, although Conservators did not address why they hired and paid a family member to provide caregiver services, and it is not entirely clear that bond should be reduced retroactively when the conservators did not request such relief at the appropriate time. No legal authority was given for either action/relief. For interim or final accountings, only the ending financial statements are required to be filed.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE NORMAN LLOYD PETERSEN REVOCABLE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #4, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Grant without hearing. The Court waives defective notice: notice mailed on 12/6/24 for a hearing on 12/18/24 was not at least 30 days' notice, as required for trust petitions. There were pages out of order in the filed petition. The disbursements schedules did not show any dates of orders authorizing payment of fees. A petition involving a trust must state the names and addresses of trust beneficiaries [CRC 7.902.], not conservatee's relatives.

rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF BERNARD M FOSTON SR
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 2/3/25 to be reviewed with the "second" accounting that should have been just an additional accounting for a reasonable period following the death of the conservatee (incorporated into the amended first and final account). Also, the amended accounting petition was e-filed on 12/5/24, which was not at least 15 days before this hearing. [LRSF 14.14 A.] A pleading (even an amended one filed to correct issues raised by the Court) should stand on its own and not "respond" to issues as though answering test questions. Issues raised by an examiner are not part of the record. It is unclear from Mr. Goralka's time sheets if the fee request is just for the unredacted time entries. Conservator's current attorneys' time sheets have not yet been fully reviewed. It does not appear that an original statement has been filed yet to verify the new CD reported on the Supplemental I&A filed on 12/4/24. Please provide courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 1/20/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF EDUARDA O. BUTAC
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #2, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. 1) A review of the small estate affidavits filed on 11/25/2024 indicates that the following additional issue may need to be addressed: According to the affidavits, Azuceno Olorvida (decedent's postdeceased nephew entitled to 1/60 estimated to be $15,854.38) died intestate and was survived by his two children: Albert Olorvida (who happened to be the petitioner and administrator) and Arnold Olorvida. However, the schedule of proposed distribution (see Exhibit A to the second supplement to the petition filed on 08/05/2024) identified Clarita Olorvida as the surviving spouse of Azuceno Olorvida entitled to share Azuceno's 1/60 share with Albert and Arnold. If Clarita has since died, an additional small estate affidavit with a certified copy of her death certificate must be filed by her successors (who may or may not be Albert and Arnold). 2) Nothing has been filed to address the following outstanding issues (e-mailed to both counsel on 08/06/2024 and posted as previous tentative ruling for 10/14/2024 hearing: i) If the correct total of Schedule E (costs of sale) is $76,734.85 (as so stated in paragraph 7 of the second supplement filed on 08/05/2024), the accounting as supplemented does not balance, in that total charges exceed total credits by $800.36. ii) The total amount of distributable net cash should be $951,810 based on the ending account balance ($1,005,738.42), less the statutory fees ($25,019.11 x 2) and costs ($3,889.93), but the schedule of proposed distribution only allocated $951,262 for final distribution. The petition has not been amended to explain the $548 deficit. iii) The schedule of proposed distribution requests a 25% deduction ($247) from Christian Olorvida's 1/960 share in favor of Kemp & Associates, but no assignment is on file. iv) The schedule of proposed distribution requests a 25% deduction ($1,585) from Dalia Encamacion's 1/150 share in favor of Kemp & Associates, but no assignment is on file. 3) The proposed order must be revised to add alternate distributees of the shares of the two missing beneficiaries, as required by Probate Code section 11603(c). 4) The Court may determine whether a 10% fee ($13,395) paid to a third party to collect the decedent's bank accounts ($133,395) escheated to the state should be deducted from the petitioner/administrator's statutory fees, as collection of unclaimed property is part of the administrator's ordinary services covered by his statutory fees. See See Estate of Billings (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 426, 430-431 ("When an executrix incurs expenses for work she has the duty to perform, those expenses may be charged against her statutory commissions. --- Using related reasoning, the court in Estate of La Motta (1970) 7 Cal.App.3d 960, 964 denied a public administrator authority to pay a third party a fee for performing one of the administrator's duties, the locating of the decedents' bank accounts. In denying the payment, the court said, "What is certain is that the Legislature never intended that the probate court have authority to order payment to a third party for services which are part of the public administrator's official functions.") rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MICHELE PAYNE
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required, although counsel's supplemental declaration failed to comply with CRC 7.703(e), in that the declaration did not describe qualifications and Business and Professions Code section 6450 compliance of the paralegal whose fees were included in the extraordiary fees. The estate asset solely consists of its royalty interest appraised by the probate referee at $0. The statutory fees payable to counsel for ordinary legal services are thus $0. However, counsel requests fees in the total amount of $8,795 for extraordinary legal services rendered to confirm the estate's royalty interest, in addition to reimbursement of costs in the total amount of $855.75. The estate has no cash or other liquid assets on hand out of which counsel's fees and/or costs must be ordered to be paid. See Probate Code section 10831(d). If counsel's fees and/or costs should be allowed, the Court may determine whether a lien should be imposed on the estate's royalty interest prior to final distribution thereof to the beneficiaries, with or without 5% simple annual interest available under LRSF 14.56, to secure payment of counsel's fees and/or costs, unless the petitioner or any other beneficiary has already advanced sufficient cash to the estate to create the payment source required by section 10831(d). rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF KEVIN KA-CHEUNG YU
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 1/29/25. Examiner needs more time to review, as she was unaware until 12/16/24 that papers had finally been e-filed to address the issues with the accounting petition raised for the hearing on 4/8/24 (the 81-page supplement and 37-page fee declaration e-filed on 11/21/24). It appears at first glance that both documents AMENDED the petition. An amending pleading requires the same notice as the pleading it amends. [CRC 7.53(a).] The only correct form of notice is the mandatory Judicial Council form GC-020. It would be very helpful if courtesy copies of these two most recent filings were provided to the assigned examiner by 1/15/25. rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF STELLA STAVROS KINGAS
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 12/30/24 for an appearance. The accounting incorporates the figures from an I&A that was fully superseded by a Corrected I&A making the accounting look incorrect and cumbersome to review as a result. The petition caption on the notice of hearing is incorrect. The administrator sold assets after the end of the accounting period, so the accounting is not a true and accurate statement of the assets on hand; sales after the accounting period apparently resulted in further losses.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF TRACEY ANN JONES
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 2/3/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) No proof of service of notice of this hearing has been filed. (2) Notice must have attached to it the procedures for how to make a remote appearance by Zoom and CourtCall, as well as remote public access, as set out at https://sf.courts.ca.gov/divisions/probate-court. [LRSF 14.1 C.6.] (3) When an accounting is presented, original bank or brokerage statements must be filed to verify the ending balance(s) shown in the accounting. None appears to have been filed. [LRSF 14.34 D.] See CRC 7.575(b) for computer-generated printouts. (4) The prayer has a different amount for the attorney's statutory fee than the amount shown in the petition. (5) The prayer also mentions a reserve, although it does not appear that a reserve was requested. (6) A petition for final distribution must state specifically how the estate is to be distributed by showing the distributee's name and description of the property, including the net amount of cash (as of a date certain) distributable to each beneficiary. [CRC 7.651; LRSF 14.35 I.6.] (7) The schedules attached to the accounting do not appear to be DETAILED, as required by Probate Code Sect. 1062: there should be a date, description, source or payee, and amount for each entry. (8) Gains and losses must show the calculation. [Probate Code Sect. 1062(d).] (9) What "trust" account? (10) Why were there disbursements to Marin County Superior Court? Please provide courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 1/20/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE DAVID C. GUTEKUNST LIVING TRUST EXCECUTED june 25, 2004, as amended and restated on Apr 14 23
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions And Costs
rulings.Rulings: (part 1 of 2) This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the Court is Petitioner David C. Gutekunst's Motion for Sanctions and Costs (the "Motion"). Petitioner David C. Gutekunst ("Movant") seeks an order imposing monetary sanctions against Respondent Lindsay Brennan ("Nonmovant") and/or her attorney Leighton Burrey ("Nonmovant's Counsel") in the amount of $27,329.05, for reimbursement of legal fees and costs incurred by Movant in this matter. (Memorandum of Points and Authorities in Support of Motion for Sanctions and Costs ["MPA"] at p. 1:20-27) Movant seeks sanctions pursuant to Cal Code of Civil Proc. sec. 128.5, on the ground that Nonmovant and her Counsel employed bad faith litigation tactics. In the alternative, Movant seeks sanctions pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2023.030, on the ground that Nonmovant and her Counsel misused the discovery process. Movant further seeks to recover costs pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. secs. 1032 and 1033.5. The Motion is DENIED for the reasons set forth below. Pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 128.5, the court may order a party, the party's attorney, or both, to pay reasonable expenses incurred by another party as a result of actions or tactics made in bad faith, or that are frivolous or solely intended to cause unnecessary delay. Section 128.5, subd. (b)(1) defines "actions or tactics" to include opposing motions or filing a responsive pleading. Section 128.5, subd. (b)(2) defines "frivolous" to mean totally and completely without merit or for the sole purpose of harassing an opposing party. Section 128.5 does not apply to discovery responses, objections and motions. (Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 128.5, subd. (e).) Movant contends that Nonmovant's objection to his Petition for Instructions Confirming Trust Amendments and Restatement (the "Underlying Petition") was made in bad faith because Nonmovant and her Counsel were both aware that Movant had the legal right to disinherit her. Movant further asserts that Nonmovant's refusal to withdraw her objection resulted in unnecessary cost and delay. Movant's request for sanctions pursuant to Section 128.5 is DENIED because Movant has not met his burden to establish that Nonmovant's conduct justifies sanctions. Movant has not established that Nonmovant filed her objection in bad faith, or that her objection was completely without merit or intended solely to harass Movant. Although Movant characterized Nonmovant's motivation as being rooted in bad faith and solely intended to harass him, Movant's conclusory allegations alone do not establish a basis for awarding sanctions pursuant to Section 128.5. Pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2023.030, the court may impose sanctions against anyone engaging in a misuse of the discovery process. Section 2023.010 sets forth examples that constitute a misuse of the discovery process, including but not limited to: (1) persisting in attempting to obtain information or materials beyond the scope permissible discovery; (2) using a discovery method in a manner that does not comply with specified procedures; (3) employing discovery in a manner or to an extent that causes annoyance, embarrassment, oppression or undue burden and expense; (4) failing to respond to an authorized method of discovery; (5) making an unmeritorious objection; (6) making evasive discovery responses; or (6) disobeying a court order to provide discovery.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE DAVID C. GUTEKUNST LIVING TRUST EXCECUTED june 25, 2004, as amended and restated on Apr 14 23
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Sanctions And Costs (Added For The Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: (part 2 of 2) Movant contends that Nonmovant abused the discovery process by asserting meritless objections and providing evasive responses, and then subsequently refusing to provide amended responses. In response, Nonmovant asserts that her responses and objections to Movant's written discovery requests were justified. Before the Court can award sanctions for misusing the discovery process, the Court must first determine that the Nonmovant's conduct constitutes a misuse. Movant's request for sanctions pursuant to Section 2023.030 is DENIED because Movant has not established that Nonmovant's conduct constitutes a misuse of the discovery process. Nonmovant's discovery responses indicate that most of the information Movant sought in discovery was contained in the attachments to court filings in the related conservatorship proceeding in San Francisco Superior Court case no. PCN-23-306910. Although Movant asserts that Nonmovant's discovery responses were inadequate and warranted further responses, the issue of whether Nonmovant's discovery responses were inadequate to warrant an order compelling further responses is not before the Court in the instant Motion for Sanctions. Pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 1032, a prevailing party is entitled to recover costs as a matter of right in any action or proceeding. Section 1032 defines a "prevailing party" as: (1) the party with a net monetary recovery; (2) a defendant in whose favor a dismissal is entered; (3) a defendant whether neither plaintiff nor defendant obtains relief; (4) a defendant as against those plaintiffs who do not recover any relief against that defendant; or (5) as determined by the court in its discretion. Section 1033.5 sets forth the items that are allowable as costs under Section 1032. Pursuant to Cal. Rules of Court, Rule 3.1700, a prevailing party who claims costs must serve and file a memorandum of costs within 15 days of service of the notice of entry of judgment or dismissal, or within 180 days after entry of judgment. Movant's request for costs is DENIED because Movant does not qualify as a "prevailing party" within the meaning of Section 1032. As applied here, Movant is the Petitioner in the instant trust proceeding; Movant is not a defendant in whose favor a dismissal was entered, and this Court declines to exercise its discretion to characterize Movant as the "prevailing party" within the meaning of Section 1032. In addition, Movant did not recover funds pursuant to the Order Confirming Trust Amendments and Restatements issued on October 17, 2024. Furthermore, Movant appears to have failed to comply with Cal. Rules of Court, Rule 3.1700, in that he has not filed a memorandum of costs. As such, Movant's request for an award of costs pursuant to Section 1032 is DENIED. The prevailing party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LYMAN GUM LEONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 01/13/2025. The following issues must be addressed: 1) No proof of publication is on file. 2) No DE-147S form is on file. A pleading cover sheet must be attached to this form. 3) No DE-121 form is on file. A copy of the remote appearance information required by LRSF 14.1(C)(6) must be attached to and served with the form at least 15 days before the hearing. 4) No original will has been filed. A certified copy of the decedent's will admitted to probate in Washington must be provided to the clerk (who then must create an original will file). 5) Item 5a(3) or (4) of the petition must be checked. 6) Item 5a(7) or (8) of the petition must be checked. 7) Item 8 must list all persons named in the will, including Cindy Leong. 8) Please provide the name and date of death of the decedent's father. 9) A "certified" copy of the order admitting the will in Washington must be filed. See Probate Code section 12521(a)(2). A certified copy of the order must be conventionally filed with a pleading cover sheet attached (cannot be e-filed). See LRSF 14.22.
